title: TTPO deprovision: staggered SSO retirement, MDM re-enroll, and courier-hold for cross-border departure
preview:
Issue summary:
HR has requested a two-week temporary passover (TTPO) deprovision for an employee relocating overseas mid-contract. Request is to stagger account deactivation so service accounts and SSO remain available for knowledge-transfer tasks until the courier confirms laptop collection. HR flagged export/compliance concerns for the laptop transit and asked Asset Management to place a hold with the courier.

Impact:
- Potential risk of accidental early SSO/account revocation interrupting handover work (engineering + documentation updates).
- Hardware custody must remain in chain-of-custody due to encryption keys tied to device KMS tags.
- Cross-border courier requires explicit KYC and export paperwork before release.

Environment:
- Internal accounts: SSO (Okta), GSuite, GitHub org, AWS console role.
- Device managed by MDM (Jamf).
- Employee tier: full-time engineering (access to staging and prod service accounts).

Requested outcome:
1) Create a hard stop plan that defers destructive deprovisioning actions for two weeks (TTPO window) and documents which identities are delayed and which are removed immediately.
2) Coordinate with Asset Management to create a courier hold (do not release) with a named carrier and tracking, including export paperwork checklist.
3) Re-enroll the device into a custody MDM profile if courier requires device reset prior to shipping.
4) Schedule final deprovision (full SSO removal, KMS key rotation, service-account handback) upon confirmation of device receipt and HR sign-off.

Background and constraints:
- Employee will be physically leaving the country on 2026-03-20; HR requested a TTPO window through 2026-03-27 to finish handover tasks.
- Security policy requires device rekey when device leaves company control for >72 hours. We need to reconcile that with the TTPO exception.
- Legal requires export checklist for this country; Asset Management has initiated a case with logistics partner but needs support from Security and Compliance for documentation.

Attachments:
- HR exit form (attached in HR drive link)
- Logistics pre-check PDF (uploaded to ticket)

Notes for assignees: please avoid immediate deactivation of roles that would break CI or production runbooks; instead rotate service account keys where possible and freeze destructive IAM role deprovision until final closure.

Impact: external stakeholders using saved permalinks from scheduled reports get 404s and cannot access traces referenced in alerts. Environment: prod us-east during a retention backfill window. Affects scheduled report links shared externally.

title: Speculative tool-schema hydration causing delayed streamed chunk delivery and mid-stream timeouts
preview:
Issue summary: Customer reports sharp increase in end-to-end latency for streamed responses when prompts include tool calls. The stream starts but streamed chunks are delayed ~2-8s mid-response, occasionally leading to client-side timeouts.

Impact: Affects multiple production customers (enterprise tier). Intermittent but reproducible with tool call enabled; increases tail latency and causes streaming sessions to abort after client-side timeouts.

Environment: prod cluster in us-east, streaming route /v1/chat/stream with toolcall preflight enabled and schema-hydration flag set to speculative=true. Observed for rw-open-7b-v1 and rw-llama-13b-v2.

Observed behavior: Initial tokens stream normally for ~200-400ms, then a stall of 2-8s while server emits heartbeats but no content chunks. Traces show a synchronous preflight/schema hydration step invoked mid-stream for tool metadata.

Expected behavior: Preflight/schema validation should either complete before the first chunk is sent or be performed asynchronously without blocking chunk emission. Streaming should not stall due to synchronous schema hydration after initial token emission.

title: Intermittent generation requests with very large max_tokens time out near token-window boundary (KV-cache refill stall suspected)
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports intermittent 504 / request timeouts for single generation requests when using very large max_tokens (typically >= 120k token window). Failures occur late in the generation (after ~70-95% of requested tokens produced). Suspected stall during KV-cache refill / eviction cycle in serving runtime when prefill and decode phases overlap under heavy batching.

Impact:
- Affects multiple request attempts from NimbusAI production workloads on Dedicated pools in us-east; blocking their long-transcript generation features.
- Symptoms: spike in 5xx (504 gateway timeouts) and client-observed disconnects; partial outputs received before termination.

Environment:
- Dedicated customer pool (dedicated), prod:us-east; models: gptx-16b-fp16 primarily.
- Requests routed through API gateway with default 60s client timeout in their code, but server-side timeouts observed beyond that.

Steps to reproduce (customer-provided):
1) POST /v1/generate using model gptx-16b-fp16 on dedicated pool.
2) Set prompt ~40k tokens, max_tokens=120000, stream=false, temperature=0.2.
3) Send concurrently from 6 clients to simulate production load.
4) Observe intermittent 504 from API gateway after long-running generation (often after ~30-50k tokens produced).

Observed behavior:
- Request occasionally returns 504 with no finalizing token. Partial stream of tokens earlier in the response.
- Serving runtime logs show long stall in "kv_cache_refill" and spikes in kernel scheduling latency.

Expected behavior:
- Long-context generation should continue to completion (or return a graceful error with a helpful hint) rather than sudden 504 mid-generation.

Customer-provided artifacts:
- Trace IDs: trace-us-east-78a4a3b, trace-us-east-78a4a3c (attached to ticket).
- Client-side logs showing request timings and partial payloads (attached).

Notes: This differs from previously reported "streaming hang" tickets because the requests do return partial tokens before failing and failure appears correlated to internal KV cache operations when requests are near the model token-window boundary.

title: Intermittent 502/503 errors during large embedding uploads due to chunked body aborts at API gateway
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer LexiconSoft reports elevated 502/503 errors and failed embedding upload jobs when sending large batched embedding payloads (~50–200MB) using our REST API over HTTP/2 with chunked transfer encoding. Errors began 2026-03-12 09:10 UTC after a gradual traffic increase.

Impact:
- Several customer embedding jobs failed (25% failure rate on a 40-job run).
- Increased API error-rate across their tenant (~P50->P95 error increase).
- Affects production P95 latency and caused retry storms from client side.

Environment:
- prod cluster in us-east-1; customer on enterprise plan with Dedicated routing to high-priority pool.
- Requests use HTTP/2 with chunked transfer-encoding and per-request streaming.
- Model: text-embedding-3-large pinned for this customer.

Observed behavior and tentative root cause hypothesis:
- API gateway logs show upstream proxy closing the request stream with 502 when the client finishes chunked body. Runtime logs show the runtime process logging: "ClientRequest aborted while reading request body" immediately followed by a worker restart in a subset of pods.
- Hypothesis: a mismatch in keepalive/read-timeout handling between apigw and runtime-proxy for large chunked uploads leads to the runtime aborting body read, causing the gateway to surface a 502. The abort path is triggering an upstream crash or worker restart under memory pressure when multiple concurrent large uploads coincide.

Steps to reproduce (internal):
1) Use a test client to POST 10 embedding batches of 1000 items each, chunked over HTTP/2 with ~10MB chunk sizes.
2) Target the LexiconSoft tenant route and run at concurrency 8 from 3 clients to simulate their sustained upload.
3) Observe apigw responses and runtime stderr. Reproduce reliably in ~4/10 runs in our staging cluster when configured with low keepalive (10s) and request read timeout of 30s.

Logs and metrics (samples):
- apigw: request_id=apigw-04b2c, upstream_status=502, upstream_message="upstream read aborted" (2026-03-12T09:17:32Z)
- runtime: [worker-7] ERROR: ClientRequest aborted during request body read (stack trace attached in ticket artifacts).
- k8s: OOM kill count on runtime pod remained 0, but rss spiked to 78% of memory before restart.
- traces: span shows request body finish event occurs after runtime has flagged EOF read timeout.

Temporary workaround provided to customer:
- Advise LexiconSoft to reduce chunk sizes to <2MB and use lower concurrency for bulk uploads. This reduces observed failures while we investigate. Customer accepted and confirmed temporary workaround on 2026-03-12 18:00 UTC.

Investigation notes:
- SRE reproduced the abort locally when apigw keepalive timeout set to 8–12s and runtime request-read timeout at 30s; extending the runtime read timeout to 120s eliminated the abort in repro.
- The runtime code path for chunked body parsing does not gracefully handle mid-stream connection resets and triggers an assert followed by an emergency worker restart in a race condition.
- The issue is amplified when multiple large uploads hit the same worker and memory footprint increases; while not OOM, the worker restarts during memory compaction cycles.
- No evidence of keystore/tls rotation involvement in initial repro.

Proposed immediate fixes:
- Backport hotfix to runtime-proxy to tolerate early client-side resets and return a 499-style response to apigw instead of triggering a worker restart. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/apigw/pull/2248 (in review).
- Adjust default request-read timeout in apigw->runtime route to 120s for large-upload routes and add a per-tenant override for enterprise customers.
- Add guardrails in runtime to avoid restart on client-abort during body read; convert assert to soft-fail with controlled resource cleanup.

Longer-term actions:
- Add end-to-end load test scenario for chunked/streamed large payloads and include in canary gating.
- Improve memory accounting for streaming workers and add an alert when rss > 70% sustained during large-upload windows.

Expected next steps and owners:
- SRE (Miguel Ortega): finish runtime hotfix review and deploy to canary by 2026-03-15.
- Runtime eng (Priya Ramesh): change assert-to-soft-fail, prepare postmortem draft.
- Support (Ava Chen): coordinate with LexiconSoft for gradual rollout and confirm fix impact.

Artifacts attached: stack traces (logs/stacktrace-221111.txt), apigw traces (traces/apigw-04b2c.json), reproduction script (scripts/repro_chunked_upload.sh).

title: Main-thread GC starvation in session-sidecar causing cascading 503s and api-gateway retries
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers observed a sudden rise in 5xx responses (primarily 503 / upstream unavailable) from the hosted API in us-west during high-concurrency streaming workloads. Error wave started 2026-03-13 02:12 UTC and lasted ~45 minutes with intermittent recovery windows.

Impact:
- LexiDocs (dedicated tenant) reported ~35% of streaming sessions failing with 503s and mid-request disconnects.
- Platform-wide p99 tail latency for text-generation increased from 110ms -> 1.8s for affected hosts.
- API gateway showed elevated retry rates causing localized threadpool exhaustion.

Environment:
prod/us-west, dedicated pool for LexiDocs. Session-sidecars (Python 3.11) present on each node to manage streaming sessions and multipart uploads. Serving runtime (Rust) remained mostly healthy on GPU hosts.

Timeline / Observed signals:
- 02:10 UTC: LexiDocs bulk streaming load begins (concurrent sessions spike + long-lived streaming).
- 02:12 UTC: Platform alerts for increased 5xx from apigw.
- 02:13-02:22 UTC: Host-level CPU on sidecar processes climbs to 95%+; we see frequent GC stop-the-world pauses sampled by eBPF traces and python-timings (>250ms pauses).
- 02:18 UTC: API gateway begins returning 503s as health checks fail to get timely responses from sidecar endpoints; retries amplify backlog.
- 02:25 UTC: Autoscaler attempts to scale but new instances inherit warmed sidecar image with same workload; partial mitigation only after 02:50 UTC when traffic subsides.

Steps to reproduce (internal reproduction recipe):
1. In a dedicated us-west pool, spin up 200 concurrent streaming sessions pinned to the same sidecar binary.
2. Each session streams ~12KB/s tokens with intermittent small prompt transformations that allocate many small Python objects.
3. Observe sidecar CPU and GC pause durations via py-spy eBPF sampling.

Logs / Metrics (high-level links):
- Grafana: https://grafana.redwood/internal/d/streams/streaming-5xx?orgId=1&from=now-6h&to=now (pan to 2026-03-13 02:10)
- Loki: query 'job="session-sidecar" level=error "healthcheck"' for 02:10-03:00 UTC
- eBPF traces attached in investigation notes (link above Confluence page)

Investigation notes:
- The serving-runtime (GPU worker) remained responsive; crash dumps show no SIGSEGV or OOM on GPU processes.
- Primary failure vector identified in session-sidecar (Python) where GC induced stop-the-world pauses blocked the request dispatcher. Sidecar implements synchronous GC-unaware loops that block on large ephemeral lists during streaming transforms.
- Healthcheck endpoint handler is served by the same thread pool; during GC pauses HTTP handlers become unresponsive causing the API gateway to mark upstream as unhealthy and return 503s to clients.
- Amplification: API gateway retries queued requests to the same unhealthy host, increasing load and further lengthening GC pause windows (feedback loop).
- Correlated grafana panel: GC pause count track overlaps with 503 spike; p95 of sidecar request duration increased from 18ms -> 620ms during spike.

Immediate mitigations applied:
- 02:30 UTC: Support applied a soft-rate limit to LexiDocs tenant at the apigw layer (reduced concurrency by ~40%); error rate dropped but not to baseline.
- 02:38 UTC: Rolled a temporary probed-sidecar image with GC tuning flags (PYTHONMALLOC=malloc, PYTHONGC_THRESHOLD adjusted) to a small canary subset (3 nodes) — canary showed 60% reduction in pause frequency.
- 03:05 UTC: Manual routing removed the most impacted hosts from the pool until restart and GC-tuned image rollout completed.

Workarounds for customers:
- Ask customers to reduce concurrent streaming sessions or increase per-session batching where possible until a permanent fix is deployed.
- For LexiDocs we offered temporary request throttling and extended credits for the downtime window.

Root-cause hypothesis (current):
- Sidecar's streaming transformation path allocates many short-lived Python objects under heavy concurrency; default GC thresholds cause stop-the-world collection that blocks healthchecks. Combined with synchronous healthcheck handlers and apigw retry behavior this produced a positive feedback loop and 503 wave.

Permanent remediation plan (next steps):
1. Engineering (/runtime and /platform) to implement: move healthcheck endpoint to an isolated thread/process (non-blocking) OR make healthchecks serviced by the runtime side rather than the Python sidecar. Owner: Diego Morales. ETA: 2026-03-20.
2. Refactor streaming transforms to use allocation-pooling or to run CPU-bound transforms in a worker subprocess that can be restarted without failing healthchecks. Owner: Runtime team. ETA: 2026-04-02.
3. Add platform-level guardrail: apigw should detect upstream GC flapping via a new health metric (GC pause ratio) and avoid aggressive retry amplification. Owner: API Platform. ETA: 2026-03-27.
4. Add automated canary rollout of GC-tuned images for sidecars when rolling worker images to reduce blast radius. Owner: SRE. ETA: 2026-03-22.

Post-incident follow-up:
- Drafting a short postmortem; planned retro meeting with Runtime and API Platform on 2026-03-17.
- Open action items added to INT backlog for healthcheck isolation and GC-safe transform patterns.

title: Edge probe split causes half-formed streamed messages after reconnect
preview:
Issue summary: Customer reports that after an intermittent network disconnect, SSE streams resume but the first resumed payloads are half-formed JSON fragments and the client parser fails. Impact: Several NexaHealth production workflows ingesting streamed clinical summaries are producing invalid partial objects; customer reports ~15% of resumed sessions encounter parse errors and drops. Environment: prod, us-east, edge-proxy path through third-party CDN probe that occasionally inserts synthetic TCP probe packets. Observed behavior: On reconnect the server sends a resumed chunk that starts mid-UTF8 sequence and omits the JSON array opener; client logs "JSON parse error: unexpected token". Expected behavior: Stream resume should deliver complete, well-delimited framing and finalization events so clients can stitch without parse errors. Business impact: High — downstream ingestion pipeline discards incomplete records, causing missing clinical notes in processing.

title: Pinned cohort unexpectedly coalesced onto legacy variant during weighted canary cutover, causing mixed outputs and cost spike
preview:
Issue summary: During a planned weighted canary cutover for redwood-v2-medium -> redwood-v2-quantized at ~2026-03-13T18:22Z, approximately 7% of Finato Bank's tenant cohort (cohort id c-88ff) that should have been routed to the canary variant was instead routed to the legacy variant pinned from a previous rollout. Impact: mixed-model responses, higher than expected token costs, and a small number of failing structured outputs for validation prompts. Severity: customer-visible degradation (inconsistent outputs) and billing spike.

Observed behaviour: traffic that matched the pin cohort showed two routing fingerprints in edge logs; a subset of requests carried a routing cookie keyed to legacy-variant-id=rv2-2025-11 while orchestration snapshots indicated the cohort should map to rv2-q1. The routing mesh appears to have merged cohort metadata during a snapshot reconcile operation which coalesced two nearby cohort keys into a single pin entry pointing at the older variant.

Expected behaviour: cohort pins created during the cutover should be isolated per rollout slice; no cross-cohort coalescence should occur.

Impact summary: - ~7% of customer sessions experienced mixed responses for ~45 minutes. - Token spend for the customer spiked by ~18% during the window (billing alert). - No requests returned 5xx, but semantic/regression failures observed on validation set used by Finato.

Quick links: - PagerDuty: PD-RED-2331 - Dashboard (cost & cohort routing): https://dashboards.redwood.internal/d/rollouts/finato-cohort-424242 - Edge logs slice: https://logs.redwood.internal/q?id=cohort:c-88ff

title: Chunked request + oversized tracing headers cause API Gateway proxy buffering and idle timeouts leading to 504s
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports intermittent 504 gateway timeouts and increased tail latency for long-running chat completions. Issue appears correlated with clients sending chunked transfer requests and very large tracing/correlation headers (X-Trace-Baggage). Impact:
- ~12% of requests from NimbleDocs in us-west-2 are failing with 504 or returning after >30s delay.
- Affects production inference for redwood/gpt-7b-v2-quant used in assistant completion flows.
Environment:
- Prod, us-west-2, Hosted API (edge -> API Gateway -> NLB -> serving pool)
- API Gateway: AWS ALB in front of Nginx proxy instances (managed by infra team)
- Customer client uses chunked transfer with streaming and attaches large tracing baggage headers (~9–12 KB)
Observed behavior:
- Requests with headers >8KB frequently trigger proxy buffering in Nginx and cause the ALB to hit idle timeout waiting for backend bytes; ALB returns 504. Other cases show 502 when header truncation occurs upstream.
- When using small headers or non-chunked uploads, errors drop to baseline.
Expected behavior:
- Chunked streaming requests with reasonable tracing headers should proxy through with streaming responses and not hit the ALB idle timeout.

title: Corporate firewall idle-probe policy terminates long-lived gRPC/control-plane sessions causing streaming interruptions in private deployment
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer (FinLedger) reports intermittent user-facing streaming breaks for long-lived chat sessions in their private deployment. Breaks correlate with gRPC errors and immediate reconnect attempts from the SDK.

Impact:
- Several production chat sessions dropped every ~3 minutes for users in us-east-1.
- Affects real-time streaming responses and some control-plane heartbeats; customers see partial responses followed by reconnect.
- Customer SLA: private deployment with 24/7 support (enterprise).

Environment:
- Private deployment behind customer-managed Palo Alto NGFW (on-prem firewall + transit GW).
- Redwood control plane + model-serving endpoints over peered subnets (10.10.0.0/16).
- Observed in prod | us-east-1 only.

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1) Start long-running streaming chat session via Redwood SDK to vicuna-13b-stream pinned model.
2) Let session remain idle (no token generation) for ~180 seconds after last activity.
3) Next client send triggers a quick failure: gRPC UNAVAILABLE or TCP connection reset and immediate reconnect.

Observed behavior:
- TCP RSTs from peer seen in packet captures between client subnet and control-plane (RST originates from intermediate firewall IP).
- Session breaks consistently around ~180 seconds of idle time.

Expected behavior:
- Long-lived streaming sessions should survive idle periods; client should send keepalives or runtime should support session keepalives so firewall state is preserved.

Logs & diagnostic artifacts provided by customer:
- tcpdump from a client host showing: "src=10.20.12.58 dst=10.10.1.14 RST" at t=00:02:59 after session start.
- Firewall session log export (redacted): shows session aged-out at 180s with action=reset, policy=inspection-idle-probe.
- SDK debug logs: last successful frame at t=00:02:50; error at t=00:02:59: "rpc error: code = Unavailable desc = connection closed".
- No evidence of server-side OOM or restart in serving runtime metrics for that pod.

Initial investigation notes (Support):
- Verified RSTs originate outside Redwood control-plane (intermediate firewall IP recorded in pcap).
- Control-plane and model-serving side show persistent sockets until RST; no server-initiated close recorded.
- Customer's Palo Alto configuration includes a custom TCP idle timeout of 180s for inspected sessions and an "idle-probe" action that injects a TCP RST for sessions it considers dead.

Hypothesis:
- Customer-managed firewall aggressively resets stateful connections after 180s idle (inspection/idle-probe). Our serving runtime keeps gRPC streams open without frequent application-level keepalives, so connections appear idle to the NGFW and are reset.

Workarounds attempted and results:
- Customer enabled SDK-level application heartbeats: sending a small application-level ping every 60s avoids the reset for several sessions. (Mitigates but not ideal.)
- SRE requested customer to create firewall exception for control-plane/model-serving subnets; customer plans to test a bypass rule during maintenance window.

Recommended mitigations / next steps:
- Short-term: advise customer to enable SDK application heartbeats or reduce firewall idle time. Documented as immediate workaround.
- Short-medium term: add TCP keepalive configuration in the private-deploy agent/runtime (configurable keepalive interval and probe count) so OS-level probes preserve state across middleboxes. See linked PR for initial runtime change.
- Long-term: update private deployment runbook to recommend recommended NGFW policies (allowlist control-plane ranges or extend idle timeout) and provide explicit guidance for inspection bypass for streaming endpoints.

Follow-ups / owner:
- Eng (networking) to review PR #1876 that plumbs conservative TCP keepalive defaults for private deploys (assignee: Diego Morales).
- Customer success to coordinate firewall exception test with FinLedger network team (owner: Priya Shah).

Resolution notes (in-progress):
- Customer implemented SDK heartbeats as a temporary mitigation and reported reduced incidents.
- Diego Morales opened INT-4382 to track runtime keepalive rollout and QA.
- Support to schedule confluence runbook update after runtime change lands.

title: Half-closed stream: trailing clause dropped when client send window shrinks during auth rotation
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer Edify.ai reports that when their browser-based client (web SDK) encounters a rapid shrink of the TCP send window (simulated on flaky mobile networks) concurrent with an opaque auth cookie rotation, the final clause of the generated stream is not returned. The server emits a half-close (FIN) path but the client-side backpressure and connection rebind cause the terminal chunk (closing punctuation + metadata) to be lost.

Impact:
- Intermittent loss of last sentence or trailing clause in streamed responses for a subset of long outputs (~>1k tokens).
- Affects a small percentage of dedicated-customer sessions in us-east under bursty mobile-proxied traffic.
- Customer-visible: generated text ends mid-sentence and no error/partial completion metadata is delivered.

Environment and context:
- Customer using dedicated capacity with reserved GPUs in us-east-1.
- SDK: redwood-js v2.8.1 (browser), WebSocket fallback to SSE for older browsers.
- Observed against model gpt-open-14b-quant pinned in customer config.
- Recent infra rollout 2026-03-10 included auth cookie rotation and an updated connection rebind path on the edge.

Steps to reproduce (reported by customer + reproduced in lab):
1) Start a long generation request via web SDK with streaming enabled (expect >800 tokens).
2) While stream is in-flight, run a client-side network shaping tool to rapidly reduce the TCP send window / simulate aggressive client backpressure and momentary socket buffer exhaustion.
3) Simultaneously trigger an auth cookie refresh (server-induced rotation) so the client must rebind/reauth mid-stream.
4) Client performs reconnect; server logs indicate a half-close on the old worker and a new session begins.
5) Observe that the final clause and the terminal metadata (choice.finish_reason / end_event) are missing from the client's assembled output.

Observed behavior:
- Server worker logs show generation progressed to completion and had emitted the final tokens, but the last chunk does not arrive at the client.
- No explicit 5xx or error is surfaced to the client; connection transitions look like normal reconnect behavior.

Expected behavior:
- Client should receive terminal chunk (final sentence and metadata) even if a rebind occurs; reconnect/resume semantics should ensure tail delivery or explicit error.

Customer reproduction artifacts attached: example SDK trace, browser HAR, and sample prompt (stored in customer ticket).

title: TS SDK: AbortController cancel + immediate retry loses tool-callback confirmations when using delegated token rotation
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer using the TypeScript SDK observes missing tool/callback confirmations after cancelling a streaming generation with AbortController and then immediately retrying. Behaviour appears correlated with delegated API key rotation in their auth stack: the retried request uses a refreshed delegated token and the previously-sent confirmation callback for the first attempt is never reconciled, leading to partial downstream side-effects (duplicate back-office jobs or missing confirmation receipts).

Impact:
- Intermittent data-loss/duplicate-side-effect symptoms in customer workflow (invoice submissions are sometimes missing confirmations, occasionally duplicated).
- Affects a subset of streams where client cancels then retries within ~2s window; estimated ~0.8% of request flow for this customer but hits critical payment paths.

Environment:
- Customer tier: Dedicated pool (reserved).
- Region: prod us-east.
- SDK: @redwood/sdk v1.7.3 (typescript bundle).
- Edge/proxy: customer uses an in-house edge sidecar that rotates delegated tokens every 30s.
- Models: redwood/fastchat-13b and openchat-7b used for orchestration.

Steps to reproduce (customer-provided):
1) Create long-running streaming generation that invokes a tool via function-calling semantics (tool emits a confirmation callback to customer webhook).
2) Immediately after tool invocation is emitted by the model, client calls controller.abort() to cancel the stream.
3) Client re-initiates the request within 500-2000ms; the edge sidecar rotates delegated token between the two requests.
4) Observe that the tool confirmation for the first (cancelled) attempt is not delivered/acked; downstream system never marks the job confirmed. Second attempt proceeds and sometimes duplicates side-effect or leaves orphaned state.

Expected behavior:
- Cancelled stream attempts should either (a) reliably surface a final state (ack or explicit cancellation) for any emitted tool invocations, or (b) be safely dedupable such that retried attempts do not produce orphaned or duplicated downstream side-effects.

Actual behavior:
- The first attempt's tool confirmation is not reconciled when token changes between attempts, appears dropped by auth layer or not retrievable by the orchestrator. No clear error returned to client. No duplicate suppression observed.

Logs / traces (redacted):
- Request A (attempt 1): X-Delegated-Token: token-abc123 (rotated soon after)

title: Multiplexed streaming 502s during mid-morning tenant burst linked to ephemeral-worker shed
preview:
Issue summary: Between 09:20 and 10:05 PDT on 2026-03-13 several customers observed intermittent 502 responses on multiplexed streaming endpoints. The errors coincided with a mid-morning tenant burst from a few heavy tenants on Dedicated pools. Impact: streaming sessions saw partial responses followed by 502s; reconnect loops caused visible degradation for ScribeAI (dedicated tenant) and a small set of other tenants. Not a global outage but high error-rate window for affected tenants.

Observed behavior: API gateway returned 502s with upstream read timeout/connection reset. Upstream runtime pods reported short-lived OOM restarts and voluntary eviction of ephemeral worker threads. Autoscaler logs show a rapid shed of ephemeral workers while persistent workers remained. Traffic pattern: long-lived multiplexed HTTP/2 streams with periodic large token bursts (embedding + generation mix).

title: traffic-diversion-to-quantized-fallback-enters-warmup-rack-causing-burst-503s
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer NovaSearch reported a sudden spike in 5xx (503) responses and p99 inference latency starting 2026-03-11 21:10 UTC. Traffic was diverted to a quantized GPU fallback variant that appears to be in a warmup/initializing state on a single rack, causing serial cold-starts and temporary capacity exhaustion.

Impact:
- Intermittent 503s and p99 latency > 12s for search reranking calls between 21:10–21:48 UTC.
- Around 18% of requests for particular routes returned 503 during the peak window.
- Customer-facing application experienced degraded search relevance and increased error rates across user sessions.

Environment:
- Redwood hosted dedicated pool (NovaSearch reserved cluster), prod-us-east-1, model routing policy uses auto-fallback: primary -> quantized-gpu-fallback -> cpu-fallback.
- Autoscaler set: min_replicas=2, max_replicas=16, scale_window=30s.

Steps to reproduce (customer):
1) Send high-concurrency reranking traffic (500 qps sustained) to route /v1/engines/rerank-nova/complete.
2) Observe 503s and rising p99 after primary model saturates.
3) Notice fallback routing to quantized-gpu variant that reports "initializing" for several seconds before reporting "active".

Observed behavior:
- Router diverted bursts of traffic to the quantized GPU variant even when the variant reported replica_count_active=0 and replica_count_initializing>0.
- The fallback variant entered a warmup queue causing serial warmups on a single rack; each new replica initialization introduced a ~6–10s coldstart window and transient 503s as worker rlimits and model load completed.
- Autoscaler did not pre-warm replicas quickly enough; routing continued to send traffic to the fallback instead of temporarily redirecting back to primary or CPU variant.

Expected behavior:
- Fallback routing should prefer variants with replica_count_active>0 and sufficient in-flight capacity and should throttle/divert burst traffic to CPU fallback or queue requests rather than pushing to initializing replicas that will 503.

Logs/observations:
- Router trace: router-id=rt-13b7, decision_time=21:11:03.124Z, selected_variant=quantized-gpu-v2, variant_state=initializing, active_replicas=0, initializing_replicas=1.
- Serving runtime (worker) logs show model load + CUDA memory reservation spikes and repeated worker process restarts during the window.
- Cloud metrics: GPU memory pressure on rack-r3 spiked to 98% when the quantized variant started loading.
- Error samples (gateway): 503: upstream worker-init-failed: model_load_timeout after 9.8s.

Impact to customer SLA and billing:
- Errors occurred for dedicated customer traffic; billing impact is likely due to retried requests. Customer flagged as high priority since reranking errors affect production search.

title: Transparent midstream proxy insertion causing serialized streaming and long tail latency between APAC and EU
preview:
Issue summary: Since 2026-03-12 03:20 UTC StreamlineAI sees high p95/p99 latency and streaming timeouts for inference requests originating in APAC and routed to eu-west. Symptoms point to an intermediate transparent proxy that is serializing HTTP/2 streams and introducing head-of-line delays; streaming responses are returned in full chunks rather than interleaved tokens, causing user-observed stalls and occasional 504s at the application layer. Impact: customer-facing production service degradation, active customer requests timing out or experiencing multi-second tail latency, increased error rates in their monitoring. Environment: production, apac-east region for client egress, destination region eu-west hosting model endpoints.

Expected behavior: streaming responses should arrive token-by-token (low TTFB, steady token stream). Observed behavior: initial handshake fine, then long pauses between token batches; tcpdump/traces show additional hop and increased TLS handshake + connect times.

title: Synchronous schema ping from function proxy adds blocking validation hop — p99 end-to-end latency spikes and 10s timeouts for dedicated customer
preview:
Issue summary: Customer ScribeAI reports sudden increase in end-to-end request latency (p95/p99) for short chat completions after a recent rollout of a validation change in the function-proxy. Impact: Customer-facing streaming sessions experience long tails and periodic 10s request timeouts. This has escalated to degraded UX for interactive flows.

Observed behavior: During short prompts (<= 64 tokens) we observe p99 latency rising from ~220ms to ~2.1s and occasional 10s timeouts. Traces show an extra synchronous RPC from function-proxy to the schema-validator service during the tool/function call orchestration. The RPC performs a schema/contract validation and an indirect K/V metadata lookup which adds blocking I/O on the request path.

Expected behavior: Schema validation should be async or performed off the hot path (prefetch/validate-on-register) so that per-request latency remains bounded. The function-proxy should not synchronously wait on an external cache miss that triggers DNS and K/V backend calls for every invocation.

Immediate impact: Affected sessions are timing out at the client (10s) and failing fast for interactive users. Customer reports a 12% drop in completed calls and is at risk of pausing dedicated traffic if not remediated.

Temporary workaround: Rolled back function-proxy change on the customer-dedicated cluster to the previous tag which removed the synchronous validation hop. Engineering mitigation applied at 2026-03-13T18:40Z. Longer-term mitigations in progress.

title: Sustained 5xx spike during large embedding upload causing accept-queue collapse and runtime preemption
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer QueryFlow reported a sustained elevation in 5xx responses (~8-12% error rate) beginning 2026-03-13 03:20 UTC during a large embedding ingestion job. Errors manifested as 502/503 from API gateway with upstream runtime disconnects and occasional runtime GPU preemptions.

Impact:
- QueryFlow dedicated tenant experienced failed embedding batches (10-20% of batches failing) and increased tail latency for chat calls.
- Customer reported retry storms and billing disputes for failed uploads.
- Incident observed primarily in us-west-2; spillover traces in us-east-1 for cross-region prefetch.

Environment:
- prod, dedicated capacity pool: dp-dedicated-42, node type: a100-80gb equivalent
- Ingress: api-gw-v3 (http2 multiplexing enabled), runtime: inference-runtime-v1.28
- Affected models: redwood-embed-v2 (batching for embedding), llama-2-7b-chat (mixed chat traffic)

Steps to reproduce (customer):
1) Start large CSV-to-embeddings pipeline with 1,000-5,000 concurrent small payloads using chunked multipart upload (parallelism 40).
2) Simultaneously run streaming chat workload with long-lived connections/keepalives (50 streams).
3) Observe elevated 5xx rate on embedding upload route and intermittent 502s on chat.

Observed logs / snippets (sanitized):
- api-gateway: "2026-03-13T03:22:11Z ERROR upstream reset by peer: connection reset while writing"
- runtime: "[watchdog] GPU preempt event: dev=0 pid=4721 reason=page_fault_retry"
- ingress: "accept_queue_drops=312 backlog=1024 conn_open=512"

title: Rapid elevation of 5xx rate during large embedding upload + priority-shed misclassification causing upstream OOMs
preview:
Issue summary: Customers reported a sharp increase in 5xx responses (primarily 502/503) starting ~2026-03-13 11:45 UTC. Several dedicated customers (notably NimbleStack) saw higher error rates on embedding and streaming routes during a large bulk embedding upload. Errors correlated with upstream runtime restarts and OOM-like preemption logs.

Impact: Multiple production customers experienced request failures and increased latency for ~45 minutes. Error rate peaked at ~18% on affected routes (baseline <0.5%). Business impact for NimbleStack: failed job completions and API retries causing billing anomalies.

Environment: prod cluster in us-east (dedicated pool A, customer tenant: nimblestack-ded-1). Affects streaming and /v1/embeddings endpoints.

Observed: 5xx increase, apigw return codes 502/503, runtime processes killed and restarted with GPU OOM flag in driver logs, kvcache eviction storms, autoscaler launching ephemeral workers which then hit GPU warmup timeouts.

Expected: Stable low 5xx floor; autoscaler should add capacity without triggering rapid restarts or prefetched cache thrash.

Initial hypothesis: Large embedding upload combined with misapplied priority-shed policy caused ephemeral acceptance of many connections into a limited pool; prefetch and kvcache contended leading to runtime thrash and gpu memory fragmentation leading to OOMs and restarts.

See 'Steps to reproduce' and 'Logs' fields for reproduction notes and relevant traces.

title: Dashboard CSV snapshot export strips row-level filters for long-range snapshots
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports that when they request a CSV snapshot export for a dashboard that includes row-level filters (RBAC-driven dataset visibility + cohort filters) over a time range greater than ~90 days, the exported CSV contains either zero rows or a dataset that ignores row-level filters (showing a global count).

Impact:
- A Tellus Health analytics team cannot reconcile exported CSVs with the dashboard numbers for monthly/quarterly reporting.
- Potential data leakage risk (exports that ignore row-level visibility) or incorrect zeroed exports for long-range snapshots.
- Blocks the customer's compliance reporting workflow; customer flagged as enterprise SLA-impacting.

Environment:
- Production account for Tellus Health (enterprise tenant) in us-east region.
- Dashboard uses nested cohort filters and organization-scoped row-level ACL via console RBAC.
- Export API called via Console "Export CSV" button and via API endpoint /v1/console/exports/snapshot with auth session token.

Steps to reproduce (customer provided):
1) Login as a tenant admin for Tellus Health in prod (us-east).
2) Open dashboard "Clinical Usage - Cohorts" which shows 120 rows for a 365-day range (filtered to their org).
3) Click "Export CSV (Snapshot)" and select Full range (last 365 days).
4) Downloaded CSV is either empty (0 rows) or contains global totals (rows for other orgs) depending on time range selected.

Expected behavior:
- Exported CSV should exactly match the dashboard table view including row-level filters and cohort restrictions for any time range.

Actual behavior observed:
- Short ranges (< 30 days): CSV matches dashboard correctly.
- Ranges 30-90 days: intermittent mismatch (row counts off by small delta).
- Ranges > 90 days: CSV is empty OR row-level filters are dropped and global data returned.

Logs / Samples attached by customer:
- Export request ID: export_20260312_84fae2 (attached as support-export-log.txt).
- Console UI session id: sess-7b23.
- API call trace: POST /v1/console/exports/snapshot -> 202 Accepted, background job job_98761.
- Job logs show: "snapshot-generator: payload window=365d, filter_clause_size=32768" then later "applier: row_filter=NULL due to predicate_truncation"

Temporary workaround:
- Customer can run range in 30-day slices and concat results; reported as unacceptable overhead for monthly reports.

title: Stateful TCP sequence rewriting by egress firewall causes gRPC frame reordering and corrupted model stream in Private VPC
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers on a Private deployment report intermittent corruption of streaming model responses (missing tokens, truncated arrays) when using long-lived gRPC streams. The symptoms began after a change to the customer's stateful egress firewall appliance last week.

Impact:
- FinSight Analytics reports production inference errors for chat sessions (several sessions per hour).
- Affects models served from the customer's private cluster (gRPC streaming generation).
- User-visible: dropped or out-of-order token sequences and occasional model shard fetch retries leading to latency spikes.

Environment:
- Private deployment in customer VPC (us-east).
- Egress path: customer stateful NGFW -> transit VPC -> Redwood VPCE.
- Traffic: long-lived HTTP/2/gRPC connections carrying multiplexed frames and range downloads for large model shards.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Create long-lived streaming chat session against private endpoint (gRPC bidi).
2) Generate responses using >= 2k token context and enable content-range shard downloads.
3) Observe session for 10+ minutes under moderate concurrency (10-20 circuits).
4) Intermittently see token order anomalies or truncated frames; shard reassembly errors appear in serving logs.

Expected behaviour:
- gRPC frames arrive and are reassembled in-order; model token stream is continuous and error-free.

Actual behaviour:
- Frames appear reordered at application layer causing JSON/stream parsing failures, partial arrays, and shard integrity mismatches. TCP layer shows no obvious retransmit bursts.

title: Embed dimension negotiation: proxy strips metadata header causing rerank CPU fallback and intermittent empty results
preview:
Issue summary: Since 2026-03-11 22:00 UTC, CortexSearch reports intermittent empty candidate sets and p95 rerank latency spikes for their dedicated pool. The symptoms started shortly after they flipped the proxy compression/fast-path toggle during a traffic spike.

Impact: Client-facing search experiences occasional blank results and multi-second tail latency for rerank calls affecting approx 2-3% of queries; revenue impact unknown but noticeable on monitoring dashboards.

Environment: Dedicated cluster in us-west, vpc-connected private control plane, embed endpoint pinned to embed-small-v2, rerank endpoint pinned to rerank-lite-v1.

Steps to reproduce (customer):
1) Send embed request with Accept: application/x-embed+proto and X-Embed-Dims: 128 to /v1/embeddings
2) Immediately call rerank with the returned embedding payload to /v1/rerank
3) Observe sometimes an empty candidate list and/or rerank p95 > 1.2s

Expected behavior: Rerank accepts the embedding payload and returns top candidates within SLOs.

Actual behavior: Around 2-3% of requests the rerank returns an empty set and the request falls back to CPU ANN path, increasing latency; logs show that the rerank service sees embedding dimension header missing for those requests.

Logs/observations: Proxy access logs show some requests are rewritten by the fast-path compressor and drop custom headers (Accept and X-Embed-Dims). Reproducer snippet attached by customer shows payload intact but missing the X-Embed-Dims header when it reaches the rerank node.

Temporary customer workaround requested: disable proxy fast-path or pin embed to explicit wire-format variant that includes embedded-dim metadata in the payload body.

title: Delayed initial token and sporadic request timeouts after runtime ABI + model variant handoff
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer IteraChat reports elevated time-to-first-token (initial token emission stalls of ~1.2s median, spike to 6-8s p95) and intermittent request timeouts (client sees 504) for chat completions after our 2026-03-11 runtime v1.9 rollout and associated model variant swap. Problem appears during session warmup and immediately after model handoff to quantized kernel variants.

Impact:
- Affected: IteraChat's dedicated pool (capacity-reserved) serving ~120 qps per cluster of rw-instruct-7b-v2-quant.
- Customer-visible: delayed message appearance and occasional 504 for chat requests; user complaints and degraded UX for active enterprise users.
- Business: Dedicated-tier SLA risk and support escalation from account team.

Environment:
- Deployment: Dedicated clusters in us-east-1 using runtime v1.9 (rolled 2026-03-11 02:00 UTC).
- Models in use: rw-instruct-7b-v2-quant pinned by customer; routing policy allowed fallback to rw-chat-13b-compat.
- Networking: Private tenant VPC with NAT egress through shared subnet.

Steps to reproduce (customer-provided and verified):
1) Start a chat session with prompt length ~80 tokens and system message.
2) Send first user message that triggers generation with streaming enabled.
3) Observe initial token emission latency and, intermittently, client-side timeout at 30s.

Observed behavior:
- Median initial-token latency increased from ~120ms to ~1.2s after rollout.
- p95 initial-token latency up to 6-8s for a subset of sessions.
- 504 rate increased from ~0.02% to ~1% for IteraChat cluster during the window.
- Metrics show a spike in per-request cold-starts and a correlated increase in kernel-selection latency.

Expected behavior:
- First token emission ~<200ms for pinned models under normal load.
- No increase in 504s attributable to runtime handoff.

Workaround in place:
- Support pinned IteraChat's cluster to runtime v1.8 and forced a warmup (pre-warming heads and KV cache); preliminary customer reports show normalization of first-token latency.

Logs/metrics snippets (redacted):

title: Sustained elevated 5xx floor for customer during mixed embeddings+chat burst: connection oscillation -> runtime GC/eviction thrash
preview:
Issue summary: Starting 2026-03-13 18:45 UTC BrightDocs reported persistent ~5-8% 5xx rate on mixed-embedding and chat routes for their tenant. Errors manifest as 502/503 from api-gateway and upstream runtime connection errors. Impact: production traffic for BrightDocs degraded, intermittent user-facing failures for document ingestion and chat features. Environment: prod-us-east, enterprise tenant (BrightDocs), traffic mix: 60% small chat requests, 40% batched embedding uploads (small/medium).

Observed behavior: When BrightDocs client increases concurrent connections and aggressively reconnects after failed requests, api-gateway 5xx floor rises and remains elevated for ~10-30 minutes before gradually recovering. During elevated period: runtime worker logs show repeated small process OOMs, kvcache eviction storms, and frequent shard reloads (cold-shard events). Apigw logs show 'upstream reset' or 'no healthy upstream' followed by 502/503 responses.

Steps to reproduce (partial/operational):
1) Simulate mixed traffic: parallel 250 concurrent chat calls (short prompts) + parallel upload of 10k small embedding batches (size ~128 vectors) across 120s window.
2) Client aggressively retries on 5xx with short backoff (<200ms) and re-opens new connections per retry.
3) Observe accept-queue growth at apigw, followed by connection churn to runtime and small OOM/restart events in runtime logs.

Relevant logs/metrics:
- APIGW traces: link: https://grafana.redwood.com/d/ingress/apigw-traces?orgId=1&var-tenant=brightdocs (request ids: req-20260313T1848-0a1b, req-20260313T1851-4f2c)
- Runtime logs: s3://redwood-logs/prod/us-east/runtime/2026-03-13/worker-23.log (OOMs and shard reload stack traces)
- KVcache metrics: kvcache.evictions/sec spike coincident with OOMs: 1200 -> 8500
- Host metrics: container RSS climb briefly before OOM, then restart and rapid reconnects

Initial customer note: "We started seeing errors after our nightly batch ramped up retries. UX shows failed uploads and chat messages not returning. Happened twice in the last 24 hours."

title: Ephemeral port aliasing across multi-NAT egress causes TLS SNI mismatch and intermittent presigned artifact 403s
preview: DatumStream private deployment (VPCE-enabled) started seeing intermittent 403s from presigned S3 URLs used by the serving runtime to stream model shards. The errors are not consistent per-shard; same shard will sometimes succeed and sometimes return 403s for the same task. Client-side retries succeed most of the time. Customer observed increased 5xxs and request retries cascading into throttling-related symptoms.

title: SSE TLS 0-RTT resumption during edge handoff produces partial markdown code fence and drops trailing list items
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer experiences truncated SSE streams when TLS 1.3 0-RTT (early data) is used and the client path migrates across edge nodes. The truncation consistently removes the closing markdown code fence (```), and drops the final 1-3 list items from enumerations. Impact:
- Customer's live documentation generator (NoteWave) shows malformed Markdown and missing bullet points in ~4% of streaming sessions during peak traffic.
- Affects user-facing web previews; breaks downstream parsers that expect closed code blocks.
Environment:
- Customer: NoteWave Labs (dedicated tenant)
- API: Hosted Redwood SSE endpoint via api.redwood.ai (prod-us-east)
- Model: gpt-redwood-3b-v1 pinned version 2026-02-25
- Network: client mobile app on LTE -> public mobile NAT -> CDN edge -> Redwood edge proxy -> serving pool
Steps to reproduce (customer-provided):
1) Use mobile app with TLS 1.3 0-RTT enabled (client uses early data session resumption).
2) Start an SSE generation request that returns multi-paragraph Markdown with code fences and an ordered list at the end.
3) Trigger a short path migration by toggling mobile data (airplane mode on/off) or causing the client IP to change during streaming.
Observed behavior:
- Stream closes without an explicit end-of-stream event.
- Final code fence closing triple backticks are missing.
- Last 1-3 enumerated bullets are dropped.
- On reconnect the resumed stream may replay a short prefix but never retransmits the missing trailing tokens.
Reproducer used by Support/SRE:
- curl -N --http3 --resolve api.redwood.ai:443:34.201.10.7 
  -H 'Accept: text/event-stream' 
  -H 'Early-Data: 1' 
  --data-binary @payload.json https://api.redwood.ai/v1/streams/generate
- Observed same truncation when simulating sudden TCP teardown and SYN from new IP within 1s window.
Logs / traces:
- Trace IDs: trace-20260312-8b4a, trace-20260312-8b4b (attached redacted).
- Edge proxy (node-edge-7) shows connection close with TLS 0-RTT early_data_aborted at timestamp 2026-03-12T09:42:21.203Z.
- Serving runtime (worker r-14) forwarded stream with end-of-stream absent; internal stream offset reached but tail tokens not flushed to client.
Hypothesis:
- Early-data (0-RTT) session resumption interacts with connection handoff logic in the edge proxy: when a RESUME + path change occurs, the edge prematurely signals half-close without flushing buffered trailing chunks for streaming SSE sessions.
- There may be a mismatch between chunk boundaries used for prefix replay and finalization token emission when early-data is used (edge discards tail CRLF sequence that marks completion).
Temporary workaround provided to customer:
- Disable TLS 0-RTT / Early Data on the client (mobile) while we investigate. Customer reports immediate disappearance of the truncation after that change.
- As alternate mitigation, switch to short polling instead of SSE for critical flows until fix is deployed.
Severity and business impact assessment:
- Functional: User-facing content corruption for a subset of streaming sessions; parser-sensitive flows can fail.
- Business: Dedicated customer on reserved capacity; risk to renewal if not addressed.
- Classification: Sev1 (P1) for this customer due to data integrity impact.
Next steps / proposed action items:
1) SRE: instrument edge proxy to log early-data/0rtt handoff events with stream offsets and buffered chunk counts (actioned).
2) Dev: add defensive flush on handoff path to ensure trailing CRLF and close-token are emitted before half-close is propagated (investigation PR drafted).
3) QA: create deterministic test that simulates 0-RTT resume + path migration with SSE and checks for markdown closure.
4) Rollout: patch proxy in a canary region and validate with NoteWave test harness before global rollout.
Investigation notes summary:
- 2026-03-12 10:20Z: reproduced by SRE on staging using synthetic early-data client; confirmed missing final tokens.
- 2026-03-12 15:05Z: Eng noted that proxy code path for early-data uses a separate chunk buffer which wasn't being drained on connection rebind.
- 2026-03-13 08:40Z: Temporary client-side toggle confirmed as mitigation.
- 2026-03-13 12:50Z: PR #452 in realtime-proxy adds drain-on-handoff and a test.
Open questions for engineering:
- Does the same issue affect websocket-based streaming (client reported primarily SSE, but websockets may share close logic)?
- Are there any model/kernel-specific interactions (KV cache flush timing) that affect the final token emission?

title: Streaming completions time out (504) when client-side HTTP/2 WINDOW_UPDATE is not sent by corporate proxy
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer AcmeFin reports repeated streaming completions from our hosted API returning 504s after long tails of silence. This appears only for some users behind a corporate network appliance.

Impact:
- ~30 requests/hr from AcmeFin experience 40-120s stalls then 504s when generating long completions (>512 tokens).
- End-user UX broken for interactive flows.
- Customer is enterprise tier; impact threatens a contract renewal conversation.

Environment:
- Prod edge (us-east) -> API Gateway (HTTP/2 enabled) -> NGINX sidecars -> serving-runtime GPU pool.
- Observed on models: gpt-open-13b, redwood-rl-7b.

Steps to reproduce (approx):
1) Customer client opens HTTP/2 connection to /v1/stream with streaming=true.
2) Server starts emitting many small DATA frames (KV-warmup + prefix output) over the stream for ~30-90s.
3) Corporate proxy between client and our edge silently fails to send WINDOW_UPDATE or stalls sending it back to our edge.
4) Our APIGW side hits server-side flow-control limits and begins buffering; write stalls accumulate; eventually the edge or NLB resets the connection resulting in 504/connection reset.

Observed behavior / evidence:
- tcpdump at edge (pid 2710) shows server DATA frames sent with no corresponding WINDOW_UPDATE from client side for ~65s (trace id f7a3...).
- APIGW metrics: per-request egress bytes continue but send queue grows; connection_write_latency spikes correlated with 504s.
- NGINX error log excerpt: "upstream timed out (110: Connection timed out) while sending request to upstream" at timestamps matching customer traces.
- No abnormal CPU/GPU load on serving nodes. Latency regression not seen for clients on consumer ISPs.

Hypothesis:
- The customer's corporate proxy (or a middlebox) implements a buggy HTTP/2 implementation that ignores or delays WINDOW_UPDATE frames under certain conditions (e.g., when TLS inspection is enabled). This prevents our edge from progressing flow-control, causing buffers to fill and the gateway to hit its connection idle/write timeouts. This is a network-level backpressure problem, not an application-level compute regression.

Investigation notes:
- Captured pcap at edge for two failed sessions and one successful session; comparison shows missing WINDOW_UPDATE frames only for failing sessions.
- Correlated with customer's proxy vendor (identified in support call) and their proxy logs showing HTTP/2 stream state transitions being held open for >60s.
- Reproduced internally by inserting a test proxy that delays WINDOW_UPDATE; identical behavior observed (write stalls -> 504).
- Confirmed sidecar NGINX configured to allow HTTP/2; keepalive and send timeouts default to 60s.

Workaround applied to customer (temporary):
1) Disabled HTTP/2 on the edge route for AcmeFin (fall back to HTTP/1.1), which eliminates the WINDOW_UPDATE negotiation and immediately resolved the 504s for their streaming requests.
2) Adjusted sidecar send_timeout to 120s for the customer route to reduce immediate resets while longer-term fix is evaluated.

Proposed long-term remediation options:
- Option A (preferred): Route affected customers to HTTP/1.1 on the edge when middlebox incompatibility is detected. Implement server-side detection heuristic for missing WINDOW_UPDATE patterns and auto-fallback.
- Option B: Introduce small server-side coalescing window: buffer small DATA frames up to X ms to reduce frequency of WINDOW_UPDATE reliance for proxies that mishandle fine-grained updates. Requires careful latency testing.
- Option C: Document recommended proxy settings for enterprise customers (TLS inspection exceptions or HTTP/2 compatibility modes) and include in onboarding checklist for Private/Dedicated sales.

Next steps assigned:
- Diego: roll out per-customer HTTP/2 fallback toggle and automation (P0 follow-up).
- SRE (L. Park): add metric/alert for "no WINDOW_UPDATE for >30s while server sending" to detect other affected customers.
- Support (Ava): coordinate with AcmeFin to engage their proxy vendor and share pcap evidence.

Status: mitigation applied (HTTP/2 disabled for AcmeFin), root cause strongly correlated to client-side proxy behavior, longer-term automation planned.

title: Payload-size throttle triggers upstream restart cycle and elevated 5xx for large embedding uploads
preview:
Issue summary: Customer BrightChat reports a sustained elevation in 5xx responses beginning 2026-03-12 02:10 UTC during large embedding multipart uploads. Impact: multiple clients seeing ~15-30% failed requests (5xx) for embedding endpoints; customer observed timeouts and client-side retry storms. Environment: prod us-east; self-serve tenant; burst of concurrent multipart uploads (~100 parallel multipart sessions from a single customer API key). Steps to reproduce:
1) Use BrightChat embedding bulk uploader with 100 parallel workers, each submitting multipart upload with ~12MB parts (total ~120MB per session).
2) Observe API gateway accept-queue growth and some requests truncated by body parser.
3) After sustained churn (~3–5 minutes), upstream serving-runtime processes restart (watchdog), causing 502/503/504 depending on stage.
Observed behavior: apigw emits 502->503 waves; logs show repeated body-parser WARNs about exceeding per-request buffer, then runtime processes OOM or watchdog-triggered restart. Keepalive socket counts increase (stuck half-closed), accept queue saturates and proxy starts dropping new connections. Expected behavior: large uploads should be gracefully rejected with 413 Payload Too Large or accepted and streamed to backend without triggering runtime restarts.
Key logs (excerpts):
- apigw: "body-parser: payload buffer exceeded; rejecting part" (2026-03-12T02:12:03Z)
- ingress-proxy: "accept_queue_len=512 -> 2048 in 30s" (2026-03-12T02:12:35Z)
- runtime: "watchdog: process pid=2345 killed due to vmem spike" (2026-03-12T02:13:10Z)
- kernel:dmesg shows oom_kill for python-worker pid 2345 at 02:13:12Z
Additional notes: customer uses a naïve uploader that sends large parts and performs retries without backoff, amplifying load. The issue appears to be a composite of apigw body-size buffer behavior, upload-service buffering strategy, and runtime memory handling for multipart streams.

title: Burst of 5xx (mostly 503/502) traced to runtime threadpool starvation from hedged streaming/prefetch traffic
preview: Issue summary: Starting ~2026-03-12 20:15 UTC we observed a sustained uptick in 5xx errors (predominantly 503 and 502) for a Dedicated customer (BrightWrite Inc.) in us-east. Errors correlate with a heavy number of long-lived streaming sessions that use hedged prefetch requests. Impact: API calls to generation/chat endpoints intermittently return 502/503; streaming sessions abruptly close; customers report degraded latency and dropped responses. Initial hypothesis: runtime threadpool exhaustion / blocking IO in prefetch path leading to upstream failures at the API gateway.

title: Catalog sync drift caused wrong model routing, quality regression, and enterprise customer escalation
preview:
Issue summary:
On 2026-03-10 at ~09:23 UTC we observed a large increase in customer-reported hallucinations and unrelated completions for Fresco Retail. Root cause traced to catalog-sync drift: the model-catalog service had an out-of-sync cache window and continued advertising an older (incompatible) variant for the 'product-description' route, causing requests to be routed to gptx-13b-v2-quant instead of the intended gptx-13b-v2 (non-quantized). This introduced repeated generation artifacts and a measurable quality regression for Fresco's production traffic.

Impact:
- Fresco Retail (enterprise) experienced elevated hallucination rate and semantic errors across catalog-enrichment requests for ~2 hours.
- API response quality SLO degraded (precision drop) and several customers opened escalations with support and account team.
- Billing exposure: some requests were served by a higher-cost quantized fallback path leading to a small invoice delta.

Environment:
- prod/us-east; Redwood hosted API; model-router + catalog-sync + serving-runtime in same region.
- Fresco's route: /v1/generate (route pinned to model alias 'fresco-prod:product-description').

Steps to reproduce (how support validated):
1) Replayed Fresco's sample request against the live router (timestamped trace id 20260310-0927-FR) -> observed model header set to gptx-13b-v2-quant.
2) Forced a catalog-sync refresh via admin endpoint -> subsequent requests routed to gptx-13b-v2 and quality returned to expected baseline.
3) Replayed the same prompt against both variants to compare outputs and token-level divergence.

Logs / Observations / Metrics:
- Catalog-sync service logs show an unsuccessful partial refresh at 09:17 UTC due to transient metadata store timeouts (backpressure). Cache TTL logic continued to serve stale entries until forced refresh at 11:30 UTC.
- Routing telemetry: model-mismatch rate spiked to 11% of fresco traffic between 09:20–11:20 UTC (baseline ~0.2%).
- Quality telemetry (prompt-eval harness): ROUGE-like and semantic similarity metrics dropped ~18% for sample prompt set.
- Billing meter reported ~3.6% of Fresco requests charged at quantized path unit pricing for the window.

Investigation notes (chronological):
- 09:25 UTC: Support received first Fresco alert; initial triage suspected quantized fallback due to GPU pool autoscale.
- 09:40 UTC: SRE ruled out GPU pool exhaustion; serving runtime healthy.
- 10:05 UTC: Instrumented model-router and catalog-sync. Found cache TTL decision had a race with incremental metadata updates (race introduced in recent change to TTL backoff).
- 10:45 UTC: Engineering reproduced drift locally by simulating metadata-store latency; confirmed wrong alias mapping persisted until manual refresh.
- 11:30 UTC: Manual catalog refresh + controlled rolling restart of catalog-sync workers restored correct routing.

Timeline (key events):
- 09:17 UTC: catalog-sync attempted refresh; metadata store returned 503 transiently.
- 09:20–11:20 UTC: increased model-mismatch traffic and customer complaints.
- 09:32 UTC: Fresco escalated to CSM and requested urgent remediation.
- 10:15 UTC: Support engaged SRE and Eng; incident declared P0.
- 11:30 UTC: manual refresh applied; routing corrected.
- 12:05 UTC: support reached out to Fresco with interim mitigation and credit offer.

Resolution:
- Immediate fix: forced catalog-sync refresh and rolling restart of catalog-sync workers to clear stale entries; added a short-term guard to prevent TTL-from-expiring-during-refresh behavior.
- Mitigated customer impact by routing Fresco traffic to pinned replica and issuing proactive credit for affected window.

Root cause:

title: Ephemeral driver IO attach lag causing reserved-pool throughput sag
preview:
Issue summary:
An intermittent surge of host-level IO activity (short-lived bursts from a background checkpoint writer) is causing large GPU attach/handshake latencies in a reserved pool. The attach delays lead to the autoscaler and scheduler seeing transient capacity unavailability, which triggers rapid slot reassignments and an observable throughput sag for the customer's reserved capacity window.

Impact:
- Streamline AI reports elevated p95/p99 latency for batched requests during a 30-90s window several times per hour.
- Throughput drop: observed 20-40% drop in steady-state token throughput for the affected partition during bursts.
- Application-level errors: a small percentage (~0.2%) of requests return 5xx attach-timeouts before autoscaler fallback kicks in.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool: reserved-cluster-14 (prod-us-west)
- Host type: 8x A100 (mainly homogeneous) with local NVMe used for checkpointing
- Agent/runtime: serving-runtime v2.3.8, driver-manager v1.9.1
- Observed windows: recurring between 09:40–16:20 UTC on 2026-03-10/11/12

Steps to reproduce (observed pattern):
1) Customer workload triggers periodic small checkpoint writes on host local NVMe (background writer every ~10–15m).
2) Within 10–30s of the write spike, host IO saturation causes elevated kernel scheduling and occasionally delayed driver attach handshakes reported by driver-manager.
3) Scheduler marks GPUs as slow-to-attach; autoscaler initiates ephemeral reassignments/fallbacks to maintain latency SLOs.
4) Result is temporary slot churn, increased p99 latency, and drop in overall served tokens until KV caches and prewarmed contexts repopulate.

Observed logs & metrics pointers:
- Grafana: /d/dedicated/pool-14/attach-latency (panel shows 10–40s attach spikes)
- Cloudwatch host IO spike metric: host-io.iops spike to 150k for 20–60s (normal ~12k)
- driver-manager log excerpt (see logs field below)

Notes: pattern is repeatable on production under the same customer traffic profile; isolated to reserved pool nodes where local NVMe checkpoint daemon is enabled.

title: Quota boundary and throttle-shepherding guidance for mixed bulk + realtime workload
preview:
Issue summary: Customer BeaconMaps seeing recurring 429s during daily bulk index refresh and low-latency routing for map tile annotations. Impact: Intermittent 429s causing failed embedders and delayed annotations in production (~18% of index refresh jobs fail).

Environment: Dedicated pool (dedicated-beacon-3), prod us-west. Heavy mixed workload: scheduled bulk embeddings (multipart uploads) + real-time annotation calls from mobile clients.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Trigger nightly bulk ingest for 80k documents via multipart upload with 16 parallel workers.
2) While ingest runs, send ~200 low-latency chat/annotation requests per second pinned to the same model variant.
3) Observe rate-limit headers and return codes.

Observed behavior: Throttles (429) occur predominantly on multipart upload endpoints and the streaming annotation calls report Retry-After of 5-7s. Customer reports that reducing parallelism to 4 workers removes 429s, but throughput drop is unacceptable.

Expected behavior: Segregated quota accounting or burst pools should allow short-lived low-latency calls to continue while bulk ingestion backfills without causing tenant-wide 429s.

Attached logs: API responses with headers, sample request traces, and telemetry timestamps provided in attachments.

Customer request: Guidance on configuring dedicated pool settings, recommended rate-window splits, and whether we can enable an isolated burst pool for long-lived multipart uploads without requiring migration to private deployment.

title: Prorated invoice line for async toolcall retry traffic not matching postback records
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports an unexpected prorated invoice line that appears to bill for ~18,000 toolcall invocations during a two-hour window on 2026-03-06. The customer asserts those invocations were retry traffic from a previous deploy and should have been tagged as test/heartbeat and therefore not billed.

Impact:
- Customer sees a +$1,250 prorated line on their 2026-03-06 provisional invoice.
- Customer is an enterprise Private deployment customer with reserved hours; unexpected charge impacts finance reconciliation and internal chargeback.

Environment:
- Peregrine Health private deployment (VPC) with hosted fallback enabled.
- Region: us-west-2.
- Affected model: redwood-gpt-large-v1 (toolcall-enabled variant).

Observed behavior:
- Invoice shows a prorated hosted-toolcall line; billing key points to hosted rate ID for toolcall invocations.
- Postback replays and reconciler logs show 0 accepted postbacks for production API key for the same timestamps; most events were marked as "retry-dropped" by the async-queue.

Expected behavior:
- Retry-dropped or test-tagged invocations should not be emitted as hosted postbacks and should not result in billed hosted-toolcall lines.

Steps to reproduce / investigation checklist performed by customer before filing:
1) Confirmed provisional invoice line present 2026-03-06 09:00-11:00 PDT.
2) Queried org postback exports for production key: zero successful postbacks in window.
3) Verified async-queue logs in their VPC showed repeated retry attempts with header X-Test-Traffic: true for a subset of connections.
4) Verified no canary alias was in use during time window.

Logs / attachments provided by customer:
- postback_export_2026-03-06.csv (attached)
- async-queue-retry-trace.txt (attached)

Requested outcome:
- Explain why invoice line was generated when reconciler shows no successful postbacks.
- If metering misattributed, request provisional credit and a root-cause explanation.

Notes for support: customer is escalated to account team; treat as high priority for billing reconciliation and temporary credit consideration.

title: Persistent 502/503 plateau from proxy TLS-handshake queueing and slow-starting upstream runtimes
preview:
Issue summary:
ScribeAI reported a persistent elevated 502/503 error rate starting 2026-03-12 02:10 UTC across multiple endpoints. Errors present as short-lived waves that plateau rather than sharp spikes — API gateway returning 502/503 while upstream runtimes report long TLS handshake queues and delayed worker start.

Impact:
- Customer-facing requests to ScribeAI (dedicated) experienced 10-20% error rate for ~3 hours during peak traffic windows.
- Streaming sessions intermittently failed to establish with 502s.
- Latency for successful requests increased by ~2x during plateau periods.

Environment:
- prod/us-east (dedicated pool for ScribeAI)
- Models: gptx-13b-v2 pinned variant
- Redwood stack: api-gateway -> ephemeral-proxy group -> serving-runtime (dedicated GPU pool)

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1. Generate steady small-payload traffic simulating ScribeAI client (60 rps).
2. Observe api-gateway logs: elevated upstream 502/503 responses, short-lived connection drops.
3. Correlate with ephemeral-proxy metrics: handshake_queue_depth spikes followed by slow worker spin-ups.

Observed behavior:
- TLS handshake queue depth increases to thousands of pending handshakes in the proxy layer.
- Ephemeral proxy threads report limited fd availability and per-thread accept backlog growth.
- Serving-runtime logs show a slow-start pattern for newly provisioned runtimes (delayed model init ~6-9s) leading to temporary capacity shortage and preemption events.
- api-gateway surfaces 502/503s (upstream reset / no healthy upstream) rather than 504s.

Expected behavior:
- Handshake queue remains low under normal concurrency; rapid accept/dispatch to runtime workers.
- Upstreams should be healthy or gracefully drain with fallback routing; no sustained 502/503 plateau.

Logs / metrics / traces (high level):
- api-gateway: errors: 502/503 rate jump at 02:08 UTC, sustained plateau until 05:30 UTC.
- ephemeral-proxy: handshake_queue_depth: 0 -> 3.2k at plateau; accept_queue_write_latency increased.
- runtime: worker_init_latency 6000-9000ms for new runtimes; repeated preemptions observed in autoscaler logs.
- netstat / kernel: no obvious ephemeral port exhaustion; socket TIME_WAIT in normal range.
- TCP/TLS traces indicate incomplete TLS handshake stages (server hello sent but no application data) corresponding with proxy accept backlog.

Immediate mitigation applied (by SRE):
- Temporarily raised ephemeral-proxy accept backlog and increased proxy thread pool by 25% to relieve handshake queue.
- Applied conservative rate-shed on low-priority tenants to prioritize dedicated customers.
- Enabled sticky routing to healthy warmed runtimes for ScribeAI to avoid cold-starts where possible.

Notes on customer communication: 
- Customer notified at 02:30 UTC that we were investigating TLS-handshake/backlog causes and applied temporary mitigations.
- Follow-up plan shared: deeper investigation into proxy accept handling, autoscaler slow-start tuning, and kernel tuning for accept queueing.
